title: Kabira
artist: Pritam, Tochi Raina, Rekha Bhardwaj, Amitabh Bhattacharya

lines.natural: What kind of selfishness is yours?
lines.chosen: What kind of selfishness is yours?
lines.translated: What kind of selfishness is yours?

lines.natural: You choose neither sunlight nor shade.
lines.chosen: You choose neither sunlight nor shade.
lines.translated: You choose neither sunlight nor shade.

lines.natural: What kind of selfishness is yours?
lines.chosen: What kind of selfishness is yours?
lines.translated: What kind of selfishness is yours?

lines.literal: On any support (place) I cannot remain fixed
lines.ambiguity: Subject is not explicit in the line; likely first person ('I') given surrounding lines.

lines.natural: What kind of selfishness is yours?
lines.chosen: What kind of selfishness is yours?
lines.translated: What kind of selfishness is yours?

lines.natural: You choose neither sunlight nor shade.
lines.chosen: You choose neither sunlight nor shade.
lines.translated: You choose neither sunlight nor shade.

lines.natural: What kind of selfishness is yours?
lines.chosen: What kind of selfishness is yours?
lines.translated: What kind of selfishness is yours?

lines.literal: On any support (place) I cannot remain fixed
lines.ambiguity: Subject is not explicit in the line; likely first person ('I') given surrounding lines.

lines.note: paigambar = 'prophet' (literal). The possessor 'apna' is ambiguous in isolation (can be 'my/your/one's own'); translated conservatively as 'your'.
lines.ambiguity: Subject/possessor of 'apna' is unclear — could mean 'I made my own prophet', 'you made your own prophet', or 'made yourself a prophet'.

lines.original: Phir bhi sookha mann ke andar
lines.literal: Yet still a dryness inside the heart/mind.
lines.natural: Yet still a dryness inside the heart.
lines.chosen: Yet still a dryness inside the heart.
lines.translated: Yet still a dryness inside the heart.

lines.ambiguity: Implied subject ('it' — the heart/mood) is taken from context; could also read as 'why are you left (like this)?'

lines.note: 'Re' is a vocative particle used to call someone in song; 'Kabira' is the vocative of Kabir (the saint/poet).
lines.ambiguity: 'maan jaa' can mean 'accept', 'agree', or 'give in'; rendered here as 'please accept' conservatively.

lines.note: Faqeera/faqeer = an ascetic or mendicant (Sufi/holy man); used here as an address.

lines.natural: Come — your shadows are calling you.
lines.chosen: Come, your shadows are calling you.
lines.translated: Come, your shadows are calling you.
lines.note: 'parchhaaiyan' literally means 'shadows' but can also suggest memories or echoes; translation keeps the literal sense.
lines.ambiguity: Could be read literally (actual shadows) or figuratively (memories/echoes calling you).
'maan ja' again can mean 'accept/agree/listen'; chosen conservatively as 'please accept'.

lines.note: ‘Faqeera’ (faqir/fakir) is a mendicant/ascetic; a cultural/religious address.

lines.original: Kaisa tu hai nirmohi kaisa harjaaiya
lines.literal: What kind of you are, unfeeling; what kind of faithless one
lines.natural: What kind of person are you—so unfeeling, so faithless
lines.chosen: What kind of person are you—so unfeeling, so faithless
lines.translated: What kind of person are you—so unfeeling, so faithless
lines.note: ‘Nirmohi’ = indifferent/unfeeling. ‘Harjaaiya’ (harjaiyā) likely means a faithless/betraying person.
lines.ambiguity: The exact spelling/pronunciation of 'harjaaiya' is unclear; it could be read as 'harjaiya' (faithless) or a regional variant.

lines.natural: A cool easterly breeze watches the road
lines.chosen: A cool easterly breeze watches the road
lines.translated: A cool easterly breeze watches the road
lines.note: ‘Purvaai’ = an easterly breeze (regional word). The line personifies the breeze.

lines.natural: The clay pitcher watches the road
lines.chosen: The clay pitcher watches the road
lines.translated: The clay pitcher watches the road
lines.note: ‘Suraahi’ is a clay/earthen water pitcher; the line personifies the object.

lines.literal: On (the) lips salt sits, not sugar
lines.natural: Your lips hold salt, not sugar
lines.chosen: Your lips hold salt, not sugar
lines.translated: Your lips hold salt, not sugar
lines.ambiguity: The verb 'rame' is nonstandard here; it’s interpreted as 'sit/are present'. There is some uncertainty about exact conjugation.

lines.note: Both are Sufi honorifics/titles: 'mast' implies ecstatic/intoxicated; 'Maula' and 'Qalandar/Kalander' refer to saintly or dervish figures.

lines.original: Bujh ke yun andar hi andar
lines.literal: Extinguished like this inside itself inside
lines.transliteration: बुझ के यूँ अंदर ही अंदर
lines.ambiguity: May mean 'fading away inwardly' or 'smothered/deadened deep inside'; exact nuance is poetic and a bit open.

lines.note: Kabira refers to Kabir, the poet-saint; speaker addresses someone as 'Kabira'.
'Faqeer' (faqir) means a mendicant or ascetic Sufi; used here as a vocative address.

lines.natural: Come — your shadows are calling you.
lines.chosen: Come, your shadows are calling you.
lines.translated: Come, your shadows are calling you.

lines.note: Re is a vocative/exclamation addressing the named person; Kabira is the vocative form of the name Kabir.
Faqeera (faqir/fakir) = an ascetic or mendicant; Re is a vocative/exclamation.

lines.original: Kaisa tu hai nirmohi kaisa harjaaiya...
lines.literal: How you are heartless, how (are you) harjaaiya...
lines.natural: What are you like—heartless, what are you—lost/defeated...
lines.chosen: How are you so heartless, how are you so lost/defeated...
lines.translated: How are you so heartless, how are you so lost/defeated...
lines.note: nirmohi = without attachment / heartless / detached. The word 'harjaaiya' is uncertain in meaning (see ambiguity).
lines.ambiguity: harjaaiya could mean 'defeated', 'lost', 'betrayed', or a dialectal/slang form; context doesn't make the exact sense clear, so the translation stays conservative.
